ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Mutton Murtabak; closest match is stuffed flatbread food product (FOODON_03315671). Murtabak is a pan-fried stuffed flatbread with spiced mutton filling.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (meat-filled pancake/flatbread) used as closest defensible match; no dedicated Murtabak code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Mutton Murtabak or equivalent stuffed flatbread dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Mutton Murtabak as served at a Singapore hawker stall or Indian-Muslim restaurant typically weighs approximately 200g and constitutes a single meal serving.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Murtabak — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Murtabak (cr_id G00010716). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Roti (Indian flatbread, pan-fried) — Glycemic Index analogous estimate based on wheat flatbread with protein-rich filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Murtabak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: pan-fried wheat flatbread (roti/chapati) with protein-rich meat filling. The substantial protein content (14.05g/100g) and fat content (11.58g/100g) from the mutton filling attenuate the glycaemic response of the wheat dough component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (60) × carbohydrate per serving (14.88g for 200g serve) / 100 = 8.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg per 100g; a 200g serving delivers approximately 934mg sodium, representing 40–47% of the 2000mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mutton Murtabak to half a piece per occasion, avoid accompanying curry dipping sauces which add further sodium, and request reduced salt in preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 7.44g/100g is low-moderate and the medium GI (estimated 60) is attenuated by high protein (14.05g/100g) and fat (11.58g/100g); however, the refined wheat dough component contributes a refined carbohydrate load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume one piece of Mutton Murtabak as part of a balanced meal, monitoring total carbohydrate intake and avoiding high-sugar chutneys or sweetened beverages alongside.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 85mg/100g, high sodium at 467mg/100g, and pan-frying in oil contribute to cardiovascular risk; mutton (lamb) contains saturated fat which raises LDL cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Murtabak to occasional consumption, preferring smaller portions, and should avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as curry gravy or chilli sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); pan-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise or avoid Mutton Murtabak and consider chicken or fish-based murtabak as a lower-inflammation alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Murtabak (also spelled Murthabak or Martabak) is a popular Singaporean Indian-Muslim street food consisting of a thin, pan-fried wheat flour flatbread stuffed with a spiced minced or shredded mutton (lamb) filling mixed with egg, onion, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, and chilli. The outer dough is stretched thin, folded around the filling, and pan-fried in oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and savoury inside. It is commonly served with a sweet-sour cucumber and onion pickle relish and a mild curry dipping sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat, and 7.44g carbohydrate, making it a protein-dense dish with moderate fat and low carbohydrate relative to its caloric density. Sodium is elevated at 467mg/100g, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. Cholesterol at 85mg/100g reflects the mutton and egg content. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. It is a staple at Indian-Muslim hawker stalls and gerai murtabak outlets across Singapore, particularly in Geylang Serai and Arab Street precincts. The glycaemic index is estimated at medium (GI ~60) due to the wheat dough component, attenuated by the high protein and fat content of the mutton filling. Clinically, it is best consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its sodium, cholesterol, and pro-inflammatory red meat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton murtabak?
What is the difference between murtabak and roti prata?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010716) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Mutton Murtabak (G00010716) as of the accessed date. The HPB database does not disaggregate total fat into saturated/unsaturated fractions for this dish entry. Trans fat was mentioned in the input health_suitability notes as approximately 0.1g/100g but this is not confirmed in the HPB per_100g data and has therefore been set to -1 pending verification. All core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. GI estimated at 60 (medium) using analogous pan-fried wheat flatbread with protein-rich filling from University of Sydney GI Database framework; glycemic load per 200g serving = 8.9. Spelling note: canonical English spelling is 'Murtabak'; input used 'Murthabak' which is a common variant preserved in aliases.
